snippets.title: Penalized loss in NumPy (conceptual)
snippets.code:
import numpy as np

# w: model weights, y_pred = X @ w
w = np.array([2.5, -1.8, 0.3, 4.1])
lam = 0.1

mse_data = 0.42  # example batch MSE from predictions
l2_penalty = lam * np.sum(w ** 2)
l1_penalty = lam * np.sum(np.abs(w))

total_l2 = mse_data + l2_penalty
total_l1 = mse_data + l1_penalty

print(f"L2 penalty: {l2_penalty:.3f}, total loss: {total_l2:.3f}")
print(f"L1 penalty: {l1_penalty:.3f}, total loss: {total_l1:.3f}")
snippets.explanation: The regularizer Ω(w) is added to the data loss. L2 penalizes large squared weights; L1 penalizes absolute magnitudes and tends to push small weights to zero during optimization.

snippets.title: Compare regularization strength on linear weights
snippets.code:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge, Lasso
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.pipeline import make_pipeline

lambdas = [0.001, 0.1, 10.0]

for lam in lambdas:
    ridge = make_pipeline(StandardScaler(), Ridge(alpha=lam))
    lasso = make_pipeline(StandardScaler(), Lasso(alpha=lam, max_iter=10000))
    ridge.fit(X_train, y_train)
    lasso.fit(X_train, y_train)
    rw = ridge.named_steps["ridge"].coef_
    lw = lasso.named_steps["lasso"].coef_
    print(f"lambda={lam}: ridge L2-norm={np.linalg.norm(rw):.2f}, "
          f"lasso nonzero={np.sum(lw != 0)}")
snippets.explanation: As λ grows, Ridge coefficients shrink in L2 norm; Lasso drives more coefficients to exactly zero. Ridge/Lasso API details are in the Ridge and Lasso topic.

snippets.title: L2 weight decay in PyTorch
snippets.code:
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optim

model = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Linear(784, 128),
    nn.ReLU(),
    nn.Linear(128, 10),
)

# weight_decay is L2 regularization on weights (not biases)
optimizer = optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3, weight_decay=1e-4)

criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

# training step: loss = CE(logits, y) + implicit L2 via weight_decay
for x_batch, y_batch in train_loader:
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    logits = model(x_batch)
    loss = criterion(logits, y_batch)
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
snippets.explanation: In neural nets, weight_decay applies L2 shrinkage during optimization—the same regularization principle as λ||w||₂² in linear models.
